And so, a lot of the fears are based on a sense that I'm not enough and I won't be loved because I'm not accomplishing enough. So, I would emphasize all that has been accomplished. And of course, Erickson talking about a baby walking is a hypnotic induction, but you can do that, but you can also talk about the real accomplishments like finishing high school, like going to college, like being able to have a relationship, like being able to get along with one's parents or whatever it is. Yeah, and so often the funny thing about people is they just don't see their own strengths. What comes naturally doesn't feel special. Right. And so, I find that over and over again as a coach or just as a person that knowing how to admire the person I'm talking to is the shift that they need to experience because they haven't seen themselves as strong before. So, for example, a woman who is in her 30s and spent all of her 20s drifting and not holding down a job and going from one country to another and having adventures has spoken to me in the past with a lot of sadness and regret that she didn't build up to anything at this point in her life. And as she spoke to me, having had a very different path myself, I was able to see the beauty of that freedom that she gave herself to drift and to know different worlds and to experience many different things. So he talks about speaking to Bruce Springsteen, about his experience before going on stage and how Bruce Springsteen explains that when he hears the crowd cheering before the concert, he feels energy that speeds him up. Another famous person who experiences debilitating stage fright that prevents him from going on stage experiences the exact same things that Bruce Springsteen does except he labels that a panic attack. Yeah, the epinephrine. The epinephrine that goes into Novacaine. And for this procedure, they had to give me quite a lot of it. So I'm a pretty calm person. I do a lot of yoga and meditation. And as I started to experience in my body, my heart racing, all the fear going through my limbs and my skin and everything, I separated from it and I thought to myself, wow, this is like a full blown panic attack. But my mind is calm. My body is out of control. And I watched. I didn't even raise my hand or try to stop them. I thought that somehow some part of me was having a huge panic attack due to this procedure, even though my mind felt calm. And I think when you're, the panic attack and anxiety maybe is about feeling like that shouldn't be unknown, like we should be able to somehow control it. Or just trying to get to the bottom of that because I think that there's something there and the meaning and there's an assumption that people have in relation to the unknown. So when Mandy went on the boat, you know, Tony put her into positions where she can't control anything anymore. She had to let go on the river. She was in a natural environment where the water was going to flow and she had to adapt to it. So then you open up to it and it's more of an excitement slash you're interacting with something that's developing rather than, oh, I have to have everything nailed down and fixed exactly my way. You know, what Tony always says that he didn't think of himself. I can't remember who said it first, but there's no courage without fear. Courage means that you're afraid and you overcome it. If you're not afraid, there's no courage. So I think what was happening in many of these ordeals is that Tony puts people in a situation where they have a physiological reaction and they don't have the option of controlling it. So they have to just go with it and while they go with it, they make an adjustment where they are able to enjoy it and let the excitement be there. And the steps that Tony suggests for us, for anyone who wants to tackle their fear is to first commit that you're ready to know that you're going to overcome your fear, to decide what you're more afraid of. So are you more afraid of losing your job or losing your relationships? Are you more afraid of being someone that doesn't take on a challenge? What are you more afraid of than the fear, than the problem? And then you tell yourself you're going to do this action, this truth, make the call, show up at the place, speak the truth of the relationship, whatever it is, you're going to do it. And he points out that the reasons need to come first and the answers come second. So you need to come up with your reasons for doing this first, then you'll come up with your answers. So it's a very important way to get leverage over yourself to overcome a fear. And overcoming a fear is just like overcoming a problem. Just the way we saw Mandy was very attached to her anxiety because it gave her so much love and attention. So unconsciously, she didn't want to have those emotions, but as soon as they started to occur, everyone paid attention to her. She was given tremendous amounts of love and support. So a pattern developed where she was experiencing that over and over again and getting all of these benefits. And we do this constantly to ourselves with whatever our deep problem, our problem that recurs, our problem that we focus on. So Mark, would you like to speak a little bit about the key decision that happens with trauma often? Yes. So we've talked about key decision is a concept that we use when we try to understand very often important decisions that people have made in the past that have been formative. So we make millions of decisions all the time, but there are certain decisions that control other decisions that we make. Like for instance, someone says, I can't trust people, or life is about this. These are very big global decisions, and then they often control people. And so there's a story that I'll take two minutes to tell it of an intervention that Tony did with the man. And he was complaining that basically his family didn't feel, he was about 50. Family didn't feel like they knew him. Everyone felt like he was very distant and withdrawn. And so Tony asked him, so tell me about one of the most dramatic things that happened to you. And he started to talk about when he almost died, when he was 19 or 20. He was a young man. He was on a train. It was going across a desert in Africa. He was in the military. And he had fallen off the train and was holding on by one hand, and he was looking down knowing that if he didn't hold on to that train and climb back up, no one was ever going to know where he went. He was just going to die in the desert. And he felt this very sense of aloneness. Now when Tony asked him what was the thought that you had right before you fell, the thought was expectation and warmth and love to be with my family, to be in the bosom of my family again. So what happens in a key decision, and very often it's a trauma like this, is when you have a sudden trauma, your brain will think whatever emotion you're experiencing before the trauma hit is bad or caused the trauma. So, and it doesn't make sense. It's just an instinctual level. So for him, the fact that he slipped and fell off the train right when he was thinking about coming and returning to his family, his brain said, you know what, you can't trust those feelings anymore. And from that time, he became withdrawn, kind of cold, and you know, methodical and survival kind of oriented that near-death experience shaped his personality for 30 years. Now in the case of Mandy here, you can also make some guesses that before her daughter had this accident, Mandy really trusted the environment there. She trusted the horses, the kids had the run of the land, there were go-karts, there were parked cars, there was all sorts of equipment, there were tools. And so whatever happened, she must have been, you know, gone to some kind of self-blame and said to herself, whatever that emotion was, it was wrong because it almost killed my daughter. And so one of the strategies that, and you use it in different situations, is to understand the key decision. When did you become like this? When did you make that decision? Tell me the story. And then you often ask afterwards, do you remember what happened right before that happened? And a person will tell you what that emotion was and then how they made that wrong for the rest of their lives. And then we have a film on this, but basically how you can then rediscover the key decision and revisit it and change it and go back. And once you understand why you made that decision, that man on the train was able to recover his warmth and his feeling of trust for his family. And just as Mandy here was able to recover her sense of that the children can be watched by the husband, that she can go on, that she can, you know, that she didn't have to hover anymore. Yeah, that's fantastic. And it reminds me of another story that I feel really moved to share with everyone about a good friend of ours who, when she was 19 years old, had a very bad accident where she was thrown from a car and ended up in a small puddle that was cold enough to keep her body alive even though she really did pass for quite some time. And in that state of dying, she experienced herself leave her body, go up into the trees. She was out in a very deserted area. And she has full recall of remembering being in the trees, looking down at herself in the puddle, watching the paramedics come and work on her and bring her back. And she felt incredible peace, incredible wholeness, incredible connection to the temperature, to the wind, to the trees, to the sky, to the night. She felt completely whole. And she actually did not want to go back into her body. She did not want, she really did not want to live. And she was brought back and had extensive plastic surgery and found herself to be, for the first time in her life, very depressed. So that experience of deep, soulful connection with the world made her want to leave the world. And then at a certain point, as she matured in her early 20s, she changed her key decision about the meaning that she gave to that connection. And she found a way in life to give back and to take care of people through her work as a massage therapist and as someone who rehabilitates others after physical trauma. And she recreated her meaning of that connection. And now she works out of joy to help people connect with that part of themselves. So I think I was just reminded of that story and wanted to share it. Great. So when we talk about loss, we're really talking about uncovering the truth because nothing that is real is ever really lost. So when you're working with clients who are either overcoming a trauma or they're saddened by the loss of a family member or the loss of a job or a position or a time in life, often people come to us as coaches because they're transitioning from having a home full of children to being empty nesters or they're leaving their childhood behind and moving into an adult life. And they need the help of a coach to make that transition. And it's very important, just like when working with trauma, that we help the client understand that nothing is ever lost. We always have that inside of ourselves, whether it was a time in life, whether it was a relationship, a job, that the truth is that there is no loss. So any thoughts on that, guys? I mean, that's where the Six Human Needs is a really useful tool because you always have a way of meeting your needs. So whatever vehicle, whatever, you know, you always have the flexibility because anything that you were doing was serving to meet a need and those needs can be met in an infinite, not infinite, but very many number of ways. And that all relationships, as well as the needs, all relationships can evolve into the next level of that relationship, even when people have gone from our life through death. We continue to have a relationship with them, just like we continue to have a relationship with our old self or someone who was in our life as a child who is now an adult. That's one of the beautiful things we can do as coaches is to help guide our clients to experience that again. Yep. That's great. When it comes to communication, one of the important points made is that as a coach, we need to not only uncover the truth, but to uncover the truth, we need to hear what's not being said. So in the case of Mandy and Scott, she's talking a lot about her pain and her trauma. What she isn't saying is, show me that you love me to her husband. She isn't saying, I'm behaving like a terrible bitch because I want you to love me. I want you to show me what's real. I want you to be here for me. So especially in our work with families and couples, we need to be the one who can talk about what's not being said. It's a powerful, powerful moment in coaching. When you say what you're not saying, but what I think I'm hearing underneath is this. And I always ask the client, does this sound right? Is this doing my understanding? Is this the truth? And there is such an incredible relief that happens in that moment when the coach is able to help bring to light what is not being said. And always with the purpose of elevating, of creating meaning, of creating love. So we would never say what's not being said here is that you hate him. That's a great point. I think what's important, I think the elevating part of it is what's most important because it's always a tricky thing with talking about a client. You don't want to paraphrase something in a way that they wouldn't understand. You don't want to be like the prototypical bad psychologist who everything you say, they turn it into a diagnosis or something that wasn't your words. They translate it into their language. But instead you translate it into an elevating language of what their intentions are, so what their higher intent is. And then by giving that words and giving that expression, the person can feel their higher intention being expressed for them, which releases a lot of fear and being judged. So now you've spoken it in a way that it can be spoken so that they can then build on it. And it can be very simple. So when one person complains about the other one's neediness, you say, you know why she or he is asking for that reassurance? It's because they love you so much. And you ask the person, is this true? You want them to remind you of this because you care so much? Or the reason you're nagging for a change in diet is because you really want him or her to live longer. And to elevate the most nagging type of response in the people you're working with by going to the deeper meaning, what's beyond that? If someone needs a lot of certainty and security in regards to their house, you can talk about what the deeper truth of that is, where that's coming from, what that need is in them. And I would say that it's even a purpose is what you're uncovering. So you're discovering why they're doing it and the purpose for it. And so what's important about that is that it disconnects them from being, it gives it a meaning and it gives it a logic so you understand. For instance, if you help it, is Mandy doing this, is she being a bitch so that her husband will love her even when she's being a bitch, then suddenly explains why she's being a bitch. And it explains to everyone, she's not naturally a bitch. This is a communication. She's trying to get something from you, then the husband, Scott now, can understand that he has a place in this. He can help her. It opens up all these positive actions that people can do. And the fact that there's an underlying purpose behind the behavior relieves the person who's having the behavior, and it helps other people understand how they can step up and how they respond in a productive way.
